claim: Agents are fundamentally an illusion because models are stateless - they cannot remember anything on their own, requiring additional components to manage state
actionable: When building agents, always implement explicit state management separate from the language model to store conversation history and tool context.

claim: The agentic process (model reasoning + tool calling + result looping) is conceptually simple and does not require specialized frameworks - vanilla code with API calls and manual state serialization works
actionable: Start building agents with simple code (C#, Python, etc.) calling LLM APIs directly rather than immediately adopting complex agent frameworks.

claim: Agent frameworks become valuable primarily when managing many tool definitions - they abstract away complexity of tool serialization and context management at scale
actionable: Adopt agent frameworks (like Microsoft Agent Framework) only when you have many tool integrations; for internal/simple agents, prefer direct API calls.

claim: Do not start with MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers for internal company agents - simpler protocols like HTTP REST endpoints are sufficient for agent-to-service communication
actionable: For internal agent deployments, use REST endpoints or simple HTTP calls instead of adopting MCP unless your specific use case requires its complexity.

claim: Context engineering (token optimization) is critical because large context windows in models degrade performance as context grows, making token efficiency essential for cost control
actionable: Actively manage context size by summarizing conversations, filtering unnecessary information, and being selective about what data you send to the model.

claim: Models degrade in quality as context window fills - do not assume that a 1M token context window should be fully utilized
actionable: When selecting a model with large context windows, test performance at your typical context size rather than assuming larger windows always improve results.

claim: There are no AI experts because the field contains too many different technologies - expertise is distributed and constantly evolving
actionable: Stop seeking single 'AI experts' and instead build teams with diverse specializations; continuously learn from technical news and ship products to stay current.

claim: The teams shipping the best AI agents are not using more magic - they use standard software engineering practices and iterative development
actionable: Focus on fundamental software engineering (shipping, iteration, testing) rather than seeking cutting-edge techniques when building AI agents.

claim: Latest models are not always greatest - smaller, older models often suffice for specific tasks like document parsing and reduce costs significantly
actionable: Start with the smallest capable model for your use case and only upgrade to newer/larger models if you encounter performance gaps.

claim: Smaller open-source models will proliferate and evolve as companies shift from expensive closed-model consumption to hosting local infrastructure
actionable: Monitor open-source model development (Meta models, Deepseek, etc.) and prepare internal infrastructure for local hosting to reduce API costs.

claim: Implementing agents company-wide requires careful change management - not just deploying the technology but fundamentally changing processes and workflows
actionable: When deploying agents across a company, plan organizational change management alongside technical implementation, not just the technology.
